I’m 36. Male. Struggle with Autism, depression, anxiety, PTSD, and I have dealt with both suicidal ideation and survived my own attempts. I was abused by my mother and neglected by my mother. Was bullied in school, and spent alot of time throughout my life alone. Not for lack of trying to make friends but something always usually ended those relationships one way or another.

I’ve moved around over a dozen times, between 3 states in the US, and 90% of those were in California.

I spent my teens and early to mid 20s living with my father (parents divorced and married new people). But left his home when his new wife was revealed to be a narcissistic drunk and tried to kill him (and he STAYED with her).

It was at that point I realized I can’t rely on family.

I briefly moved back in with Mom, but moved in with a friend in Oklahoma because Mom doesn’t change (still hasn’t).

The relationship with said friend became complicated when we started becoming intimate and she decided to keep me at arm’s length and I would find out towards the end I was being financially abused. She also didn’t pay her half of the rent so we were nearly evicted. We severed ties.

I still had to leave that apartment and stayed in a hotel for a month. (She got to stay in the same apartment complex even those there was proof of what she did, so I don’t know where the Justice is there)

I moved in with another friend only to realize too late that that friend was a narcissist with BPD & a psych degree. Was treated more like a toy or pet than anything there. Was promptly kicked out when I confronted her.

Became homeless for over a year, spent 8 months in a homeless shelter, was hospitalized for mental health a handful of times.

Found my faith though, so there’s that.

I came back to California in September of 2024, but upon seeing again, nothing has changed here with Mom, even after I have spent over 20+ years of therapy, Mom never went to therapy.

She refuses help and the responsibility of her actions.

And she fails to see the damage it caused me…

And what it has done to my younger brother.

He has a far more severe form of autism (mind of a 3rd grader) and while he actually has had a job, he is now very likely about to lose said job due to him losing his temper multiple times. (Where do you think he learned that behavior from? *sarcasm)

And I’ve hospitalized myself twice since coming back here.

I’m still her punching bag.

And honestly…?

I’m done.

I left once. And next month, I’m doing it again.

Homelessness at least means I only have to worry about myself and rely ONLY on myself.

We do what we have to in order to survive.

I’ve had brushes with death.

And honestly, would rather take my chances out there.

I’ve had to make peace with this. With what this means. That no member of my family can be trusted. That I can’t turn to whatever is left of my friends either. I’m on my own.

The only difference between the 1st time I was homeless and now…? (Aside from some prep time) Is that I’m more at peace with it this time around.

Feels more like a choice. I mean it’s a rock and a hard place kind of thing, neither options are ideal.

But neither is living a miserable half life when you’re trying to repair your mental health and move forward only for your abuser & your past to be in your face day in and day out.

Nobody wants this. Either option.

But sometimes you play the hand you’re dealt.

As for me, probably leaving California again. Taking the rest of the month to gather supplies and decide a destination, then I’ll take it one day at a time from there.

That’s all I can do at this point.

So, brother, while I haven’t been where you are, remember, it can always be so much worse.

I do wish the best for you and will keep you in prayer!

Good luck!

The water crisis in Cape Town has been caused by a combination of neglect if maintenance and expansion of infrastructure by the National Government, run by the ANC, as wel as Mismanagement of the water stock in the reservoirs or dams feeding Cape Town, by the Wetern Cape Profincial Government and the Cape Town Municipal Government, both run by the DA government.
When the ANC took over government in 1994, SA had reserve electricity and construction of more dams were planned to provide for the shortages experienced in many parts of the country during drought periods. Since 1994, SA's population has doubled, mostly thanks to illegal immigration from all over Africa by people fleeing wars and dictatorships, looking for a brighter future and the ANC has frantically been connecting the ever increasing population to the existing electricity and water supply but has stopped maintenance on existing infrastructure and planned expansion there of was halted. No new dams were built and soon the power stations were not able to produce the amount of electricity they were supposed too, leading to large industries leaving SA and millions of people becoming unemployed. As water pipes were not replaced at the end of their life time, an ever growing percentage of water simply leaked out before it reached the consumers. Burst pipes in suburbs, causing thousands of kiloliters to run down streets into storm water drains, became the order of the day. Ofcourse the expanding population connecting to a declining supply would eventually lead to a crisis.

The DA which won local government and municipal elections in the Western Cape and in the City of Cape Town, is responsible to manage the reservoirs in the province and the City, but after the water crisis have already existing, not only they allowed more water to farmers than their quota while, citizens of Cape Town were constantly being scolded for not saving enough and the Mayor did house visits with TV cameras to shame alleged perpetrators, but sluices and valves of various dams were opened, letting valuable stored fresh water run out into the see. As day zero approached, and residents were allowed to spend no more than 4 minutes in the shower per day, using a bucket to catch whatever water they can to use in the toilet, the City dragged its feet negotiating over priced contracts to construct badly placed deselenation plants with capacity which would be a drop in a bucket of the demand. As citizens started uncovered all of this and were getting ready to que daily with buckets to collect water from tanker which were planned to transport water into the city, suddenly water was released from a reservoir up stream, owned by farmers who receiver good rain and shortly after, the drought ended with a La Nina event, filling all the dams within one season, just in time not only for Cape Town, but for many Cities and towns across South Africa which were approaching the same crisis.

So, don't be ridiculous and blame the climate. Water is a valuable commodity in SA and needs to be conserved, but we have always had droughts before and we will continue to have them. If infrastructure is not maintained and expanded in line with demand, you will eventually run dry. This time the rain came in time, but soon enough the next drought will come around. In the meantime, the population is growing, infrastructure is not expanded and the same people are in charge of managing the resources. Can someone see a disaster coming and does the climate have anything to do with it?

I keep watching this video when I am tempted to call my mom, or email her. The accusations she's made ring in my ears, and I am always afraid that somehow I've been too harsh. One of the last things she said was "Don't make me the bad guy!" and "Don't let 'them' brainwash you!". Intellectually I know these are irrational fears, paranoia. That I was always trying to make what she said fit my positive view of her, that I ignored statements that other people would've ended their relationships over. Still, her words pop up in my mind sometimes. I feel a pang of doubt, and I have to revisit the last conversations we had, and it is again extremely obvious I did the right thing.

I want to talk to her again someday, but I know I'm not ready yet. It has only been in the last few months (I haven't spoken to her since May) that I have started to see my confidence blossom.

The last time we spoke was my last attempt at having a real relationship, one where she does't require that I hide the parts of myself she doesn't like. I begged her. I said I needed to have a real relationship, not one that can only exist if I hide and edit myself into oblivion whenever we were together.

I still struggle with calling what she was doing (and honestly continues to do through emails) "emotional abuse". When I think of abuse I think of someone hitting someone else, or at the very least, calling them stupid or ugly. My mom was always saying I was beautiful, that I was smart. She once told me I was the main thing that kept her alive after my father passed away, she said she couldn't abandon me. 

Later, though, she lumped me (or rather the things about me my mother hates) in with my father dying, her cancer and other horrible life events. At one point she said she wished I had died rather than left the faith. She accused me of being/doing things she is opposed to just to be different, to defy God or, more generously, because I'd been brainwashed. 

Sigh. Now I'm generally ok. Living my life, on my own, like she'd always insinuated I couldn't handle, despite telling me I was smart and talented. I had to give up any help from her, leap into things. It was terrifying, but it was something I should've done years ago. Her financial help had given her power, and many times she mentioned her gifts when things got heated. THAT is emotional abuse, and then you feel horrible or conflicted and guilty, confused, ashamed, convinced you can't do things on your own. 

I had been living with her since I graduated last May, to "save money". 

Now I pay my own bills. I work hard and try to make time for my passion projects and a social life. When I mess up (e.g. forgetting a bill or over drafting) I hear her voice, "I told you so". Still, I'm beginning to realize that messing up is kind of part of it. You learn that way. It sucks but that's life.

I still think of her too often. Things feel so unfinished, painful. The last email she sent said, "So have you abandoned your family? What's the deal?" 

Something I do that helps is to try to imagine what I'd do in this situation, and compare how she deals with it. If my daughter suddenly cut off communication with me for five months, telling me in an extremely long text message that she was hurting, felt that she couldn't be herself around me, that the words I'd said caused her to feel unsafe in my love for her... I would tell her:

"I love you very much. I'm sorry if I hurt you. It breaks my heart that you feel that way. I in no way ever want you to feel that you have to hide yourself or lie to make me feel comfortable. Even if I disagree with choices you make you have the right to make those decisions and to be yourself around anyone. Please, whenever you feel ready, call me. I love you."

The way my mom reacted was to literally tell me the life I suggested just POSSIBLY ending up in was "not happening. It's just not." Literally telling me that my will about my own life was irrelevant. How much more dehumanizing can you get?

Then she said that it was perfectly natural for children not to "flaunt" things in front of their parents they didn't want to know. I told her I wasn't asking to flaunt it, but I was sick of literally and figuratively hiding.

She did offer an apology at the very end of our last conversation. "Well I 'm sorry. Ok? We need to start over." I asked her, what was she sorry about? "Everything, ok? Just let's start over." I asked, ok well do you still want me to hide all the parts of me you don't like. She said yes. That I was being irrational to insist upon it. That I was basically being a drama queen. 

Five months ago, after I first cut off communication, she threatened a childhood treasure (my harry potter books) while passive aggressively mentioning she was in town if I wanted them or she'd throw them away. After that, short utilitarian messages about bills asking if I was going to the family reunion etc. 

Nothing personal. Nothing real. Nothing vulnerable. Just controlling. 

So yeah. It's hard for me to say "emotional abuse" but it's really and truly what it is. I think until I can call it that comfortably I probably shouldn't try communicating with her again, but it can be very hard.

there are a few things I take issue with here.
firstly, " despite unions being banned", unions were legalisd in the 1820s, and they were widespread beforehand, so it's dubious to say they had no hand in increase wages during the industrial revolution. in fact the data suggest that legalisation spurred wage increases due to greater bargaining power. Indeed, it's one's power to assert a stake to the economic output that is probably the only real determiner of wealth distribution, if we're being real.

Greater productictivity does make products cheaper per unit, that is true, but it doesn't take away the fact that ownership of productive assets is monopolised by a small section of society. Without access to this, the majority of mankind is dependent on selling its labour to make a living, which is vulnerable to inflation and technological changes.
Let's not forget also the fact that most of our commodities are produced in "developing" countries where labour laws are even worse, so yes exploiting poor people does make a lot of cheap products for western markets and improve profit margins, but it is not sustainable, and western countries are already being hollowed out as a consequence.

"Stock buybacks transfer capital resources from companies who can't find as marginally valuable uses of the money as those the investors think they can, raising total value-added" That's patently false, this channel just did a video on how amazon has become so fantastically successful by reinvesting its profits into small side businesses that need capital support, in other words, companies that no investor would bother investing in. There are always ways of reinvesting, THOUSANDS of ways, but not all return a profit.

Why have real wages stalled for decades relative to the increasing yields from assets? why has the middle class shrunk in most countries? why are so many unable to afford a house? why is one person's income not enough to sustain a family? why are people up to their eyeballs in debt to get a degree? Sure, the marginal cost of producing

I find it interesting that people keep claiming rent is increasing because of supply and demand. Looking at my area, the population has been declining every year since the 1980s. When those people left, they didn't take their houses and apartments with them, which means there should be an increase in supply and a decrease in demand every year. On top of that, they are still building new houses and apartment buildings which adds even more to the supply. Yet the prices just keep going up and up. And every time a new apartment building gets built, the rent for all the other rentals in that neighborhood increases even more. The biggest increase I've seen is when covid hit. 1 bedroom apartments suddenly went from $600 a month with heat included to over $1000 a month with nothing included, and houses went from being about $150k to $350k. No one will ever convince me that it's a supply and demand issue.

. . . Born with ADHD (Late life diagnosis at 46). . .
. . . Good diet for the first 16 years. Then all down hill from there. I never learned how to cook.
. . . Bulemic from age 17 to 27.
. . . High Caffeine, High Sugar for MANY YEARS. . . still with high stress.
. . . Breast Cancer in 2020.... Yup, during COVID (note: I loved COVID! Teaching from home? ...it was fun! Not having to interact with the phonys at work was awesome! Downside. . .we had to physically go back to work and "play the game" ... I would have liked to stay at home.
UNFORTUNATELY, I didn't fully realize most of it. I was too busy surviving.

2020 - 2023 I was working 18 hours a day, 7 days a week to "sort of" keep up. Very Hard. And even though I had been honest with all of my diagnosis, I was bullied into retirement by the State of MN at the end of 2023. (I had been a loyal, hard working, college math teacher for over 30 years.) That event was life changing. . . Very Bad, but also, SUPER GOOD. Then, My parents passed in the spring of 2024. (April and June)

Now my life is much better. No family left. No more guilt. No more manipulation. No more games. And I have had the time to learn how ADHD has shaped my entire life. I have been eating Healthier since June 2024. It took 6 months to clear my brain fog and start to regain my brain functioning control. (Artificial SUGAR can send me back into brain fog Very quickly.) So, I eat a high protein, no caffeine, loooow sugar diet every day (natural sugar). I see a nutritionist monthly and am slowly building healthy habits. I have cut my depression meds in half. I use a magnesium supplement to help with sleep and quit taking Ambien. I still use Clonazepam, but only at night to help my brain quiet down. The change is unbelievable. (I never bought into the cell phone hype until 2019. BUT, I do not have it glued to my hip. I spend 1 hour every evening responding to voice mails, messages, and emails. I refuse to be at anyone's beck and call.)

My parents are divorced and I switch between houses every week. My mom is fine, she's nice to me, but whenever I'm over there, I'm just worrying and crying thinking about the fact that I have to go back to my dad's house, with my dad and my stepmom. It seems, every time I come over, I've done something wrong. If my stepmom's bought me clothing without me knowing it and I don't like it, I'm yelled at. If I'm a few minutes late coming over, I'm yelled at. Every thing I do, they're watching and judging me. They act nice and sweet to me when we're around other people, but whenever we're alone, they're scolding me and putting me down. They only tell me they love me after they've sent me crying to my room. They borrow my money without asking, and it makes me really uncomfortable. I told them how it makes me feel and they screamed at me for more than two hours about how I'm ungrateful, disrespectful, and don't love my family. My stepmom's a teacher at my school, so I can't even escape her there. She forces me to stop being friends with the people I like and become friends with the people she likes. If there's something I'm interested in that they think is a waste of time (drawing, ukelele, acting, etc.), they stomp all over it, crushing any hopes I've had of finding something that makes me feel happy, and instead force me to do things I hate, because they want me to (badminton, robotics, band). If I make even the smallest mistake, I'm demeaned and scolded, even if it was completely accidental. Most nights I cry myself to sleep because everything they say and do overwhelms me to the point where I start to believe it. Whenever something good happens to me, I feel happy, but it doesn't last more than a heartbeat before my thoughts come crashing down again, invalidating it and making me feel even worse than before. I can't talk to the people I actually like because, whenever I try to approach them, to call them, or even to send them a text message, I start to panic and think about everything wrong with me. I think about how they'd never like me, because after all, what's to like? I avoid my own friends because my mind won't stop screaming the horrible things that I know they hate about me, and even though I know it's my fault, it hurts, because my friends don't talk to me anymore. They say to cut negative people out of your life, but I can't. I could probably talk to someone, change the rules so that I wouldn't have to go there any more, but I won't. My mom has no money, no job, and three kids. My dad and stepmom have lots of money, and have already saved thousands of dollars for my post-secondary education. If I leave, they'll just take that money and give it to my brother, and I'll have nothing. And I hate that, because it's really selfish of me, but if I can just wait it out a few more years, I'll be out of their hands and finally be able to shut them out from my life, but I don't know if I can make it that long. Each week it gets worse, I feel worse. But I'm not brave enough to get help, I'm not brave enough to stand up to them, and I'm not brave enough to take my life, so instead I'm just waiting, letting my life fall into shambles and hoping that it'll be over soon. When I'm not in pain, I'm completely numb inside and I don't know how to fix it.

I come into this both understanding the need to try and preserve the local culture and the fear of too many new people coming in while also trying to have a steady stream of new faces who can integrate and carry the traditions on. I'm from what was once a smaller rural mountain town in America, with my family being the oldest in the town from before it was first settled and was a working area for the railroad. It's somewhere that before I was born tried too harshly to resist changes but then became too flooded with new people when it opened up very rapidly and completely ignored the complaints. Now it feels like a losing battle where us locals are fighting to keep the culture and important parts of our town alive while waves of new homes are being built separate from us (but all around the town) without the local culture. They don't come to the festivals at all and they openly look down on the locals as poor and stupid country people.

I think what these communities are doing is wrong and its going to end off killing their traditions because they want nobody. In the same way our traditions are slowly dying before my eyes from too many new people coming and not attempting to integrate with us at all. Its a very hard thing watching your local customs die off more and more each day, I hope these towns in Japan can wake up and make the right decisions to turn it around. But sadly I think for many it will need to be after some of these old bullies holding onto control are gone too.

That stat is technically true but very misleading. The “40% of all federal income taxes” figure only looks at federal income tax, not the full tax system. Most working- and middle-class Americans pay a much higher share of their income in payroll, sales, and state taxes, which aren’t included in that number. Plus, many of the ultra-rich don’t even show much “income” on paper — they live off asset-backed loans and unrealized gains, which aren’t taxed at all. So while the top 1% pay a large share of income tax, the system still allows them to grow massive untaxed wealth and pay far lower effective rates than teachers, nurses, or small business owners.

1950’s Baby Boom, work, wife home teaching children, school optional in many areas.

You raise good points in this video... but real world timelines dispel the cause-effect relationship you're asserting. The job-hunting reality - as you illustrated well - has been that way since mid-2022, I'd say. This subtle, steady decline in the job market has been well masked by a stock market bolstered by ai funding and unemployment metrics that can't differentiate between the gig economy and true employment. But the prolonged nature of this recession is weird in that it's more difficult to attribute it to a specific event or policy. For example, the combined layoffs in Tech equal if not exceed those that resulted from DOGE, which points to something more systemic, like AI. Yet, the more I research AI, the more I believe its impact (up to this point) has been largely overstated and used as a scapegoat for companies that already wanted to do mass layoffs.

In short, I don't know what's causing our predicament. I, like others, am simply left with a sense of existential dread about my future employment prospects. And that's depressing.

Not out of fear for myself but because they are often scared and attempting to protect themselves and my behavior might scare them and trigger their fear reactions.  It’s so complex because they need help but the average well meaning person isn’t trained to handle unpredictable mental health issues. We often assume drugs and alcohol but mental illness is just as intertwined.

I live in what would be considered a "higher affluent" district in the PNW. The K-12 per annum budget for the district per-capita comes out to $28K PER STUDENT. This, in an area where the average classroom size is 28. Do the math. $784K per classroom. About $120K of that completely covers the instructor salary, full benefits, and PERS funding (public employees retirement system). The remaining $664K PER CLASSROOM is for "other" expenses.

To give some perspective on just how much "overhead" is involved in this district, consider this: A student living at home and attending their senior year at the HS "costs" $27K. That same student the following year, still living at home, can then attend college at the state university 15 miles away. Including all tuition, parking, fees, books, etc. It costs HALF the K-12 budgeted per-capita. In short, it costs twice as much to send them to K-12 as it does to attend college.

"Overhead" indeed. And, naturally, they're still screaming for more money EVERY year. And every year state and nationalized academic review ranks the students lower and lower. We're spending more than ever for an end-product that has never been worse. You'll see a 76% graduation rate walk across the stage for their diploma, and if asked, maybe a dozen of the could balance a bank account. @LC-sc3en I don't disagree that we are part of this world and consequently there isn't really a clear distinction between things human and things of nature. And if we keep the earth a place able to sustain life, than that's obviously in our own best interest.
I'm just trying to make 2 points: 1) It doesn't help mixing things up when we speak of them. Yes, red is also kinda related to green, because the absence of certain wavelength in green light makes red light red, but it doesn't help anybody to speak of it like that. If we don't distinguish between topics, we soon won't be able to talk about them in any other way than something-in-the-world problems. And 2) People out there who become an activist, actually try to do something, will always pick one or two things that they consider most important, most urgent, most in need of their action. There are too many issues in the world for any single person to get up in the morning and think "I'm going to fix the world, now." The best thing you can expect is "I'm going to fix this part of the world, now." And I don't blame people for doing that, I don't think there is any other way to get stuff done, rather than get muddled up in all the millions of things that actually need fixing. So, what I'm trying to say is: It doesn't help to ask Greta Thunberg why she doesn't fight against the gender paygap. She's already fighting a battle. There is too little of her to fight ten at the same time. Such questions are just another form of whataboutism. And I can say that this kind of attitude has always stopped left-wing movements in Europe from getting anywhere, because all the people who were trying to make the world a better place always derailed into arguing about how and where to save the world best and who you must not be seen with while saving the world. I appreciate anybody who takes up action to make the world a better place. And if they're doing it saving the whales, then that's great. They do that and I can focus on other things.
(And personally, when it comes to racism, I just don't care if it comes in mint or strawberry flavour. Really, really disappointed with this video. I'm not sure if it's political or economic biases, but this was a a very flawed analysis of the issue. As with all economic analyses, it's based on numerous assumptions and perhaps this is where you went wrong, but it's hard to tell since you didn't outline your assumptions from the onset of the discussion. There was little to no consideration for corporate lobbying & negative externalities brought on to society from corporations, or government redistribution of wealth, which are all very important factors to consider when discussing income inequality. Also, your approach comes off as being even more laissez-faire than even Adam Smith who recognized that in a free market economy, businesses receive no benefit to educating it's workforce and will fail to provide a basic education, which is an issue I strongly believe we are beginning to experience in America today as government pushes legislation that favors private interests over public interests (e.g. public education and healthcare). As far as I can tell, you're assuming everyone has equal opportunity to innovate and become an entrepreneur, which is again obviously a flawed assumption. Wealthy elites who inherit businesses didn't create wealth, they just inherit it and in doing so have access to opportunities far beyond that if the average citizen. Additionally, access to education across various incomes is greatly unequal, but again this is another aspect you don't even acknowledge or take into account. Instead, you attempted to convince me that I'm better off today than I would be had I lived during the Renaissance or dark ages, which is already obvious, and I don't need anyone to tell me this. It's more so about recognizing whether there are ways in which society could be better off than it is today through managing wealth inequality. As such, I'm wondering wheres your consideration for research on something like a universal basic income, because that could help provide further insight into the issues surrounding income inequality as well. I recommend taking another stab at addressing this issue because this first attempt is flawed.

And obviously we weren't allowed to water our gardens or wash our cars.

But as the vid said, it rained. And oh boy did it rain. From the beginning of the drought up until this year, our dams have gone from 20% average capacity to 90% capacity.

I hope we have learned our lesson and take water consumption extremely seriously going forward.

### *How the U.S. Welfare System Compares to Argentina’s (Spoiler: Both Have Big Problems)*

The original text dives into how the **U.S. welfare system got hijacked by corporations**—private companies skimming profits off programs meant to help the poor. Meanwhile, Argentina takes a more government-run approach, but it’s not perfect either. Let’s break it down.

---

### *1. Privatization: When Welfare Becomes a Profit Machine*
#### *United States*
- *Corporate middlemen**: Companies like *Maximus and Liberty Tax handle welfare programs (Medicaid, food stamps, housing aid), often **cutting corners to boost profits**.
- *Sketchy incentives**: These firms make money by **denying claims**, delaying services, or even **kicking people off benefits* to save costs.
- **Inflated prices**: In housing programs (like *Section 8*), landlords jack up rents because they know the government will pay.

#### *Argentina*
- *Government-run**: Programs like *AUH (child benefits) and pensiones are **direct cash transfers**, no corporate middlemen.
- *Some private involvement**: NGOs and contractors help, but **profits aren’t the main goal* like in the U.S.
- *Less corporate abuse**: Since the state calls the shots, there’s no *Liberty Tax-style loan sharking off welfare checks.

**Key difference**: The U.S. outsources welfare to **profit-driven companies**; Argentina keeps it in-house (but deals with politics and red tape).

---

### *2. Welfare Programs: Who Actually Gets Help?*
#### *United States*
- *Medicaid mess**: Private insurers and dentists **overbill for unnecessary procedures* (like baby root canals—yes, really).
- **Food stamps (SNAP)**: Now electronic, which cut fraud, but companies still take a cut.
- *Foster care scandals**: Firms like *Maximus pocket money meant for foster kids by rerouting funds to states.

#### *Argentina*
- **Free healthcare**: No corporate gatekeepers, but hospitals are overcrowded.
- *Food aid**: Direct subsidies (e.g., *AlimentAR cards) with *less red tape* than U.S. food stamps.
- **AUH**: Cash for poor families, **no corporate middlemen skimming profits**.

**Bigger issue in the U.S.**: Companies turn welfare into a **business model**, while Argentina’s problem is **underfunding and bureaucracy**.

---

### *3. Who’s Getting Ripped Off?*
#### *United States*
- *Predatory loans**: *Liberty Tax lures poor folks with "instant refund" loans at crazy interest rates.
- *Medicaid fraud**: Dentists and dialysis centers **bill for fake treatments* (like pulling healthy teeth).
- **Foster kids’ money stolen**: Private firms divert federal aid from orphans to state coffers.

#### *Argentina*
- *Political pork**: Some welfare programs get used as **vote-buying tools* (e.g., mayors favoring supporters).
- *Kickbacks**: Overpriced contracts for food/medicine, but **not systemic corporate scams* like in the U.S.

**Similarity**: Both systems leak money—but in the U.S., it’s **corporations cashing in**; in Argentina, it’s **politicians playing favors**.

---

### *4. Does the Money Even Help?*
#### *United States*
- *Spends a ton* ($1 trillion/year on welfare!), but *efficiency is garbage* thanks to corporate bloat.
- *Poverty dropped in the 1960s* (thanks, Medicare!), but then *stalled* as privatization grew.

#### *Argentina*
- *Big spending too* (~5% of GDP on welfare), but inflation and crises **wipe out gains**.
- **AUH helped poor kids**, but didn’t fix deep poverty.

**U.S. problem**: Corporations **suck up funds**.
**Argentina’s problem**: Bad economies **outpace aid**.

---

### *The Bottom Line*
| *Issue* | *United States* | *Argentina* |
|-------------------------|-------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| *Who runs welfare?* | Corporations (profit first) | Government (politics first) |
| *Biggest flaw* | Companies exploiting loopholes | Political corruption, underfunding |
| *Does it work?* | Not really—too much waste | Helps, but economy keeps screwing it up |

**Takeaways**:
- The U.S. **needs to stop letting corporations turn poverty into profit**.
- Argentina *must cut political games* and stabilize its economy.
- **Neither system is perfect**, but the U.S. model lets businesses get rich off the poor—which feels extra gross.

**Final thought**: Imagine if both countries took the **best parts of each system**—Argentina’s direct aid + America’s funding (without the corporate vampires). A pipe dream? Maybe. But worth fighting for.

---

I have personally learned of one here lately, my business really took a hit during COVID, but we really buckled down, worked 80-100 hours a week, no more vacations, we haven't bought clothes in 5 years we put all of our effort into saving our business. The final blow was we had a business loan go from 6% interest to 36% interest. It effectively pushed us into chapter bankruptcy. Which is kind of like debt restructuring, where you pay back debt over 5 years at 5% interest. The interesting part came when our appraisals for our house and commercials buildings went into dispute by the state trustee, and honestly the value of everything we had submitted was being contested. The trustee hired several appraisers to come out and evaluate our property, some of these appraisals came up hundreds of thousands of dollars higher then previous appraisals, in the 200-400% higher range. We'll come to find out the trustee makes 8% of everything she is able to get from us through the bankruptcy process as commission. So she is incentivised to get the highest return possible. And she can use state funds to make sure that happens. So no money out of her pocket but more chess pieces to make sure she gets the highest commission possible. Also they can force you to sell assets, one lady had 35k in debt and went to court for 4 years because the trustee was trying to force her to sell her house, that lady spent more on the lawyer protecting her house then the original credit card debt she owed. One case I heard the state spent a million dollars in state funds investigating someone who owed 800k dollars in debt. In our case we either continue working our selves to death for 5 years because the trustee expects us to maintain our income, if we make more, they take more. If we make any less they will force us to do s chapter 7 and take everything we own and have worked for for 20 years. I have a house that has been payed off for 20 years. We put my wife's disabled sister in for free, no rent so she could have a place to live. They are taking that because it is not a profitable asset and there fore a luxury item. In the paper work the realtor will get 6% for the sell, and the trustee will get 8% for the sell, plus several thousand for paperwork and the rest will be divided out to my creditors. It's a wild process majority of people whom enter in have no idea what they are getting them selves into. That's supposed to help people in bad situations but in the end might be worse then any other path. If you have any money they will make sure to strip you of all you have.

I had been with my ex for over ten years before I broke up with him last year. For the first few years we were together, things seemed fine. But after we started college, he became clingy and wanted me to spend ALL my time with him. He'd call me at night, but do all of the talking, and when I had no response he'd me to have one.
Near my twentieth birthday, I'd had enough of this and tried to break it off. However, I had tried this at school with no one in sight, and as a result he chased me around campus, not allowing me to get to my class and wrestling my phone away from me when I had tried to call my grandfather to pick me up. I did not attend class that day, and later I foolishly forgave him and allowed the relationship to continue after he promised to be less clingy.
During the next five years, things seemed all right, but there were times when he questioned some of my decisions. In 2013, I had decided to start cosplaying as a male character at conventions. When he heard about this, he asked me some rather insensitive questions, like: "you're not becoming trans, are you?" or "wouldn't you feel better dressing like a girl character?"
Another time he had tried making my career decision for me, saying I should become a freelance artist instead of try to get a job at an animation studio.
By last year, he had gone back to being clingy, and was angry whenever I would draw for myself or my sister, going so far as to say "are you working on that stupid drawing for your sister again?" If I called him out when he would do things he would yell at me for, I was automatically in the wrong.
I remember looking up signs of abuse after being struck by him while suffering an anxiety attack, and seeing a page on emotional abuse, and the pieces fell into place as to what had been happening. I had talked with my sister about it, and she encouraged me to break it off for good, since she had been aware that something was wrong for a good while.
Since then, I have been trying to pick my life back up, slowly but surely. There have been so many things that I haven't been able to accomplish because of him, including getting my associate's degree, and it hurts to know that I could have done so much in my life sooner had this relationship never happened.

Forget about the Fox News/Trump crowd. They're not engaged in a genuine conversation either. They want the status quo to stay as is and they'll engage in whatever disingenuous tactics in order to make that happen. But that doesn't mean everyone else is with you or against you. I don't want discrimination or harassment to happen either, but I'm also concerned about the way we seem to be going about addressing this and the ambiguity around what constitutes the problem in the first place. Workers aren't (yet) featureless robots. They have a great variety of ways they interact with one another, and while some behaviors are clearly not ok, there's a large grey area of healthy human behavior which if interpreted uncharitably could be made to seem problematic. And the fear is that this movement is arbitrarily grouping those behaviors into the same bucket as the obviously problematic ones and since you often don't know where the line is but could be blamed for crossing it anyway, it could feel easier to just try to avoid situations in which these interactions could occur altogether, whether this involves avoiding women or just being waaay less natural and open around women. I have a few female friends in my friendgroup. I'm aware that I act pretty differently around them than I do when I'm just with my closer male friends. I don't feel as comfortable to be myself, talk about the same topics, (and we don't even do the kind of "locker-room talk" that you'd associate with this discussion.) or make the same jokes. And that's kind of unfair to both myself and them isn't it? I don't really know for sure if they'd be generally ok to behave the same way I do around my male friends, but when the potential risk isn't just offending/losing a friend but potentially bringing in social, legal, or quasi-legal judgement into the situation, it just doesn't feel worth it when I can always fall back on the friends that make me feel more comfortable being around. This almost feels like it's own kind of discrimination. Behavior that would be generally acceptable around men is potentially unacceptable around women. It's sort of like the "but for..." logic of discrimination law. The described behavior wouldn't be a sufficient justification for punishment "but for" the sex of those involved. So unless we want to get rid of all forms of human behavior and interaction which don't fall into a narrow band of behaviors necessary for basic work functions, we're going to have situations where some normal behavior rubs up against our problems with gender norms, and if our solution is to problemetize these behaviors only when it involves how a man behaves toward or around a woman, then we're just creating a new kind of discrimination which curtails the behavior of one group and potentially deprives another group of equal access to social capital.

Supply and demand. They were called baby boomers because they had a ton of kids. More people seeking housing = housing prices go up. A ton of people also died during WW2 and Vietnam, so there were more houses than people that needed housing until Gen X and older Millennials grew up. Crazy amounts of immigration don't help the problem, either.

Same with wages, supply and demand. More people available to work = lower wages. If nobody was willing to work for pennies, employers would have to raise wages, but with more workers than available job openings, companies get to pick and choose. There is always someone willing to do the job cheaper, especially when they come from a place where living with their parents and grandparents is normal; they aren't concerned with earning a wage that would allow them to live alone.

People like to blame those that buy second homes and corporations for the housing prices, but even collectively they don't have as big of an impact as the increase in population does on supply and demand, and they have zero affect on wages.

People getting married later in life or not marrying at all is also part of the problem. In the past, people would get married in their early 20's, meaning two people only required a single house after moving out of their parents. Now people get married in their 30's and 40's, meaning they require individual housing. With married people both being expected to earn an income, that also increased the price of housing and lowers wages. You will notice that current income is about half of what it used to be, adjusted for inflation... because two people per household are expected to work.

This affects rent as well as home prices, but an additional factor in home prices is that ever changing building codes and the permitting process makes building new houses more difficult and expensive, to the point that builders can only turn a profit if they build 'luxury' housing. There are no starter homes being built anymore due to the low profitability. For example, in my area, all new housing requires solar panels, backup batteries, EV chargers, high-efficiency HVAC, and a fire sprinkler system. Adding those things individually isn't a huge deal, but all together they increase the price of a new home by 50-75k over one without those things. This indirectly increases the price of older homes, because they will still sell as long as they are cheaper than the newer ones.

A few years ago, the IRS began reporting data on the 0.001% of returns ranked by AGI, in Excel files named NNin03.etr. , where NN is the last two digits of the calendar year in question.
In 2014:
* The number of such returns was 1396. The total AGI of these returns was $207B, 2.1% of total AGI;
* 327 of these returns were filed by idle rich, defined as returns reporting no wage/salary income. These returns include those of rich widows;
* These returns reported $39B of dividend income. Only 40 of these returns reported no dividend income;
* 1379 of these returns reported a total of $127B of capital gains, a form of income that is nonrecurring. Realised capital gains are usually used to purchase new investments;
* Around 700 of these returns reported a net business loss;
* All but 19 of these returns itemised deductions, which totaled $33B;
* These returns reported $174B of taxable income and paid $50B of income tax, 3.6% of all income tax paid;
* These returns claimed $1.1B of tax credits;
* The effective tax rate on these returns was 24%. I would prefer that this rate be increased to 30-35%.

There is no reason to give such attention to such a rarity aside from protecting male fragility and the notion that men can no longer exert their dominance as they please.
"However, research suggests that the majority of false claims do not name an alleged perpetrator – they’re more likely to be relatively vague accusations about a stranger. False allegations also tend to be identified very early on in the investigative process, often by an admission from the complainant. Given this, the widespread concern that false allegations are rife, that they damage the life and reputation of the innocent, is often a red herring." See: http://theconversation.com/heres-the-truth-about-false-accusations-of-sexual-violence-88049 and loads of other academic research that corroborate this. Due to the stigma that surrounds reporting these incidents and crimes, it's quite possible that the false allegation rate is even lower.
